name: Cycad

symptoms.name: Vomiting, Diarrhea, and Abdominal Pain
symptoms.onset: Rapid; typically within 15 minutes to 4 hours of ingestion.
symptoms.notes: ◦ Vomiting is often the first sign. ◦ Diarrhea may be bloody or appear as "black, tarry feces" (melena), indicating severe irritation and hemorrhagic gastritis.Liver Failure (Hepatic Necrosis)

symptoms.name: Acute Liver Failure, Jaundice (Icterus)
symptoms.onset: Delayed; typically develops within 48 to 72 hours.
symptoms.notes: ◦ Signs include yellowing of the skin and eyes (jaundice), increased thirst, and fluid accumulation in the abdomen (ascites). ◦ The toxin cycasin causes liver cells to die (hepatic necrosis), which is a primary cause of death in affected animals.Neurological Abnormalities

symptoms.name: Seizures, Tremors, and Ataxia (Wobbly Gait)
symptoms.notes: ◦ The neurotoxin beta-methylamino-L-alanine (BMAA) is responsible for central nervous system effects. ◦ Symptoms range from muscle tremors and weakness to paralysis, coma, and death.Abnormal Bleeding (Coagulopathy)

symptoms.name: Bruising (Petechiae/Ecchymosis), Nosebleeds, and Bloody Stool
symptoms.notes: ◦ As the liver fails, it cannot produce necessary blood-clotting factors, leading to spontaneous bleeding and visible bruising on the skin or gums.Systemic Weakness

symptoms.name: Lethargy and Depression
symptoms.notes: ◦ Cats may appear unusually tired, weak, or unresponsive as the toxins affect multiple body systems.

chemicals.name: Cycasin (a glycoside)
chemicals.chemical_formula: Not explicitly provided in the sources.
chemicals.description: in cats: ◦ Metabolic Activation: Cycasin itself is not the direct toxic agent; once ingested, it is hydrolyzed (broken down) by bacteria in the cat's intestinal tract into the active toxic component called methylazoxymethanol (MAM). ◦ Hepatic Necrosis: This active metabolite (MAM) targets the liver, causing profound hepatic necrosis (death of liver tissue). This leads to severe liver failure, jaun
chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ Highest Concentration: While found in all parts of the plant (leaves, stems, bark, roots), cycasin is most highly concentrated in the seeds (nuts). ◦ Extreme Lethality: The seeds are exceptionally potent; the ingestion of just 1 to 2 seeds can be fatal to a pet. Initial symptoms can appear within 15 minutes to several hours, but liver failure typically develops within 48–72 hours.

chemicals.name: Beta-methylamino-L-alanine (often abbreviated as BMAA)
chemicals.chemical_formula: Not explicitly provided in the sources.
chemicals.description: in cats: ◦ Neurotoxicity: BMAA is a neurotoxin that affects the central nervous system (CNS). ◦ Neurological Symptoms: It is primarily responsible for the neurological signs observed in poisoning cases, such as weakness, ataxia (wobbly gait), muscle tremors, seizures, and paralysis.
chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ Presence: This compound is present alongside cycasin in the plant tissues. ◦ Synergistic Effect: While cycasin causes the fatal liver damage, BMAA contributes to the debilitating neurological symptoms that often accompany Sago Palm poisoning.

treatments.name: Induction of Vomiting and Administration of Activated Charcoal
treatments.description: If the cat is neurologically stable (not having seizures or tremors), the veterinarian will induce vomiting to remove plant material from the stomach. This is followed by the administration of activated charcoal to bind toxins in the gut.
treatments.notes: Because the toxins in Cycads undergo "enterohepatic recirculation" (where toxins are reabsorbed from the gut back into the liver), veterinarians typically administer multiple doses of activated charcoal. A common protocol involves one initial dose with a cathartic (to speed up passing) followed by t

treatments.name: Administration of Hepatoprotectants (NAC and SAMe)
treatments.description: Veterinarians administer specific medications to protect the liver and aid in its repair. These include N-acetylcysteine (NAC) and S-adenosylmethionine (SAMe).
treatments.notes: Since liver necrosis (death of liver tissue) is the primary cause of fatality in Cycad poisoning, these drugs are essential to support liver function. Long-term care with these medications may be necessary if hepatic necrosis develops.Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy and Glucose Support

treatments.name: IV Fluid Resuscitation and Dextrose Supplementation
treatments.description: IV fluids are administered to maintain hydration and support blood pressure. If blood tests reveal low blood sugar (hypoglycemia)—a common complication of liver failure—dextrose (sugar) is added to the fluids as a constant rate infusion.
treatments.notes: Fluids are critical for 48–72 hours to support the body while monitoring for liver failure. Hypoglycemia can lead to seizures and coma, making glucose monitoring and supplementation vital.Symptomatic Neurological and GI Management

treatments.name: Seizure Control, Anti-emetics, and Gastroprotectants
treatments.description: Medications are given to control specific symptoms as they arise: methocarbamol for muscle tremors, anticonvulsants for seizures, and gastroprotectants (stomach coating meds) for severe vomiting or bloody diarrhea.
treatments.notes: Severe gastrointestinal signs (like bloody diarrhea) and neurological abnormalities (tremors, seizures) are expected with Cycad toxicity. Controlling these symptoms is necessary to stabilize the cat while the liver attempts to recover.Clinical Monitoring (Coagulation and Chemistries)

treatments.name: Bloodwork and Coagulation Profiling
treatments.description: The veterinary team will perform daily blood tests to monitor liver enzymes and check the cat's ability to clot blood (coagulation profile).
treatments.notes: Liver failure can lead to coagulopathy (bleeding disorders). If this occurs, the cat may require plasma transfusions or specific interventions to manage spontaneous bleeding.

emergencyNote: Induction of Vomiting and Administration of Activated Charcoal
safetyNotes: Symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea (often with blood), severe lethargy, jaundice (yellow skin/eyes/gums), abdominal pain, neurological signs including seizures; liver failure develops within 24–72 hours.
Seeds are the greatest danger: One or two seeds can be fatal. The entire plant — leaves, roots, and seeds — contains toxic compounds.
Action: This is a life-threatening emergency. Seek immediate veterinary care if any part of the plant has been ingested — do not wait for symptoms.
description:
Cycads, including sago palm (Cycas revoluta) and related Zamia species, are extremely dangerous ornamental plants for cats. They are among the most liver-toxic plants known in veterinary medicine and should not be kept in homes or gardens where cats have access.

The main danger comes from a toxin called cycasin. After a cat eats the plant, this substance can be changed by gut bacteria into a compound that severely damages the liver. Cycads may also contain compounds that can affect the nervous system, such as BMAA. All parts of the plant are toxic, including the leaves, stems, roots, and seeds, but the seeds contain the highest levels of cycasin and are especially dangerous.

If a cat eats any part of a cycad, early signs may include vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, loss of appetite, and low energy. More serious signs can follow, such as jaundice, abnormal bleeding, weakness, seizures, and acute liver failure. Even a small amount of seed material can cause severe and potentially irreversible damage, and the outlook may still be poor even with aggressive treatment. Homes with cats should completely avoid keeping cycads. If you suspect your cat has chewed or swallowed any part of the plant, especially a seed, contact a veterinarian or emergency animal hospital immediately—do not wait for symptoms to appear.
